California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornrnission
June 25, 1991
Peter E. Tracy
Town AttOrney
Town of Mammoth Lakes
P.0. Box 485
Bishop′  cA  935■4
Re:  Your Request for lnformal AsSiStance
Our File No. I-9■
-083
Dear Mr. Tracy:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mamnoth Lakes' Mayor Gordon Alper regarding his
respons ibi I ities under the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the
PoLitical Reforn Act (the tr46;rr;.1 Since your advice request does
not refer to a specific governmental decision but seeks general
quidance, we are treating your request as one for inf orrnal
issistance.2 The advice provided is prospective on)-y and is not
to be construed as advice regarding past conduct-
OUESTIONS
1. Must Mayor Alper disqualify hi:nseIf e/ith respect to town
council matters concernJ'ng develoPment projects on which he has
bid or with respect to other projects of the developer?
2. tlust Mayor Alper disqualify himself wi-th respect to town
council matters concerning Persons who have been sources of income
to his retail glass shoP?
1 Gorr".n*"nt code sections 81ooo-91015. A1I statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornrnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations section
18OOO, et seq. Al.l references to regulations are to Title 2,
Divj.sion 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section
83114,' Regulation 18329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804Ю 807 ● (916)322´ 5660


Our File No. I-9■
-083
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. where a developer appears before the town council with
respect to a project on which the mayor has bid, the mayor may not
participate. 
However, the mayor rnay participate with respect to
other projects of the develoPer.
2. uayor Alper may not participate in any decision in which
a source of- incorne of S25o or nore in the preceding 12 nonths is
directly involved. Moreover, l'layor Alper may not participate in
any decision which will indirectly naterially affect a source of
income of $25o or rnore in the preceding 12 months.
FACTS
!,tayor Gordon Alper owns and operates a retail glass shop as a
sole proprietorship within the Town of l'lammoth Lakes. customers
hire him to repair and replace broken windows, screens, entrance
doors, shovrer doors and other items needing glass- The cost of
his services varies. 
However, in some cases customers may pay him
up to S1,ooo for services during a 12-month period.
In addition, the mayor contracts as a glazer. Generally the
mayor will pl,ace a bid on a project and, if selected, will sign a
coirtract to perform the work. The developer may appear before the
town council while the rnayor's bids are under consideration. The
mayor has become concerned that he has a conflict of interest !'ith
respect to these develoPers.
ANALYSI S
I. 
Econonic fnterests
The Political Reform Act (the nAct"), was enacted by the
people of the state of California by initiative 
in 1974. The
purpose for the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act was to
Lnsure that public official-s, whether elected or appoi'nted, would
perform their duties in an inpartial Inanner, free from bias caused
by their own financial, interests or the financial interests of
persons vrho have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any pubtic official 
fron making, participatinq in
making, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financiaL i,nterest. A "public official" 
is defined in section
82048 and Regu],ation 18700 (copy enclosed) as every natural person
who is a member, officer, enployee, or consultant of a state or
locaI government agency. This definition would include the nayor
of Manmoth Lakes.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial i.nterest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiII have a uaterial financial effect,


Our File No. I-9■
-083
Page 3
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official 
or a member of his or her irnrnediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
***
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a cornmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($zSo1 or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tine vJhen the
decision is rnade.
Section 87103 (a) and (c) .
In your letter you stated that ljlayor Alper owns a retail
glass sh6p in the iuiisdiction. 
clearly the nayor's business is
in econornic interest. 
In addition, any person or business that
has nade any palrment to uayor AIPer or his business in the past
12 nonths is I lource of income to Hayor Alper for the purposes of
Section 87103.
The fact that the customers provide the mayor incone through
his sole proprietorship would not change the character ization of
the payment. Section 82030 provides that the income of an
individual also includes a pro-rata share of anv incone of anv
business entitv or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
qreater. 
Thus,
or nore to the
l.layor Alper .
those persons that are sources of income of $250
mayor's business are also sources of incone to
If any individual or business has been a source of income to
the rnayor of $250 or nore within the 12 months prior to any
decisi.on under consideration by the town council, that person is a
potentially disqualifying. econonic interest, and Mayor Alper may
not participate in any decision that will reasonablv foreseeablv
have a material financial effect on that person,
However, Section 87103.5 provides a Linited exception to the
conflict of interest laws for income frorn rrretail sa1es." Section
87103.5 provides that a retail customer of a business entity
engaged in retail sales of goods or services to the public nay not
be treated as a source of incorne to an official 
who owns the
entity, if the retail, customers of the business entity constitute
a significant segment of the public generally, and the arnount of


Our Fi■e No. ■-9■-083
Page 4
income received by the business entity from the customer is not
distinguishable from the amount of incone received from its other
retail customers. (See also, Regulation 18703.5, copy enclosed. )
You have not provided us eith sufficient facts to determine
if the exception applies. Therefore, ve must leave this factual
deterrnination to you and uayor Alper. we have enclosed sone
sample letters for guidance. (Hertz Advice Letter, No- I-90-663;
Novoa Advice Letter, No. f-90-313; ltontna Advice Letter, No. I-89-
515i Cook Advice Letter, No. A-89-401, copies enclosed. )
rI. 
Forseeabil itv
As stated above, a public official 
is required to disqualify
himself from participatinq in a decision in which the official 
has
a financial interest. 
This wilt occur if the deci-sion will have a
forseeable and rnaterial financial effect on an econornic interest
of the official. 
whether the financial consequences of a decision
are reasonably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is
made depends 6n the facts of each particular case- An effect is
consideied reasonab)-y foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certaintv is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops' 198,
copy enclosed. )
In Thorner, the Marin l'lunicipal i{ater District was confronted
with a decision as to uhether a district 
$/ater moratorium should
be lifted. 
The removal of the Doratoriun uould have a drarnatic
effect on developnent projects in the district, 
and ur. Thorner
asked whether there would be a confLict of interest for Director
Jack uacPhail if he had bid to supply building naterials for one
of the developnent proj ects.
The Commission determined that liftinq 
the noratorium would
foreseeabLy affect Director MacPhail's business if he had bid or
,a" pr"p.r-ing to bid on a contract with the developers to supply
mateiials, or was likely to supPly materials to the developers.
Thus, the rule set forth in Thorner is that lrhere the business
entiiy j,n which the officiat 
has a economic interest makes a bid
on a contract for a specific Project or is preparinq to rnake a bid
on the project, decisions.which affect the project will have a
reasonably- foreseeabLe financial effect on the business entity.
consequently, we would conclude that where a developer
appears beiore the town council with respect to a project on which
the mayor has bid, it is foreseeable that the decision will


Our File No。 ■-9■-083
Page 5
financially affect the mayor's business. However, it is not
foreseeable that decisions concerning other projects of the same
devel.oper would have a financial eff6ct on tire irayor's business.3
This same general rule of forseeability would apply to
sources of income that do business before the tolrn council. 
where
a source of income is an applicant, there wiII be a certain effect
on the source of income. where the source of income may only be
indirectly affected, the financial effect on the source of incone
is forseeible if there is a substantiat likelihood that it will
occur .
III. 
Materia I itv
Regulation La702.1 (copy enclosed) provides that the effect
of a de6ision is rnaterial if any business entity in which a public
official 
has an econonic interest is directlv involved in the
decision before the pubtic official's 
agency. A business is
directly involved in a decision before the town council when the
business entity, or its agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision wil,I be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or sirnilar request or,'
(2') Is a naned party in, or the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official 
or the official's 
agency;
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702. 1(b) .
Thus, lrhere a source of incoue (or the nayor's business) is
an applicant or is the subject of a torrn council decision, the
effect j,s presumed to be material and the nayor nay not
part ic ipa te
However, where a developer apPears before the tolrn council
wi,th respect to a project on which the mayor has bid, the mayor's
interest (his glazing business) lriIl not be directly involved.
Moreover, in many cases, town council decisions wiII have effects
3 Yo.l. f acts do not
an economic interest
has been a source of
developer is also a
forth in this letter
indicate that the rnayor would otherwise have
in the developer, such as where the developer
incone to the mayor. Obvj.ously, where a
source of incone, the alternative analysis set
nust be applied.


Our File No. I-9■
-083
Page 6
on a variety of interests in the community, even though they are
not directly involved in the decision.
Under the Act, an econo:nic interest rnay still 
require
disqua I if ication with respect to a decision if the economic
interest rnay be indirectly rnateriatly affected. whether the
indirect efiect on a business entity, such as the nayor's business
or business-entity clients of the mayor, is material depends on
the financial size of the business entity. 
For exatnple,
Regulation L87o2.2(gl (copy enclosed) provides that for a
relatively srnalI business entity, the indirect effect of a
decision is materi.al where:
(1) The decision wilt result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of S10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 92,500 or more,' or
(3) The deCiSion will
or decrease in the Value of
of S■0,000 。r more.
result in the increase
assets or I iabi l it ies
ConsequentJ.y, if a town council decision concerning a project
on which tfre rnayor has bid wiII affect the gross revenues of the
mayor's business by $lo,ooo or nore for a f iscal' year, or the
vaiue of assets or- Iiabil.ities 
by Slo,ooo or more, and subdivision
(S) is the appropriate standard, the mayor nay not participate'
i5reover, if- Lne decision will result in the mayor's business
incurring or avoiding additional exPenses or reducinq or
eIj-rninating existing expenses for a fiscal year in.the anount of
S2,5oo or ior", the-mayor nay not participate. 
This sane analysis
would be apptied to businesses which are sources of income to the
rnayor.
Where a source of income is an individual potentially
indirectly affected by a town council decision, Regu)'ation
La702.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is naterial as to an
individual who is a source of incorne or gifts to an
official 
if any of the foJ-Iowing applj,es:
(a) The decision will affect the
individual's incorne, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by 9f,000 or nore;
or


Our File No。 ■-9■-083
Page 7
(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a man-
ner that is considered material under section
L8702.3 or Section 18702.4.
However, even if you deterrnine that uayor Alper has a
financial interest that l{i11 be financially affected by the
decision, Mayor Alper nay still 
participate if the effect on Mayor
Alper's interests is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. 
(In re ov/en (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77, .copY
enclosed. ) This is necessarily a fact-based test. 
Since we have
not been provided with the facts of a particular decision, we can
not advise on this issue. we have enclosed rnaterial for your
inforrnation. (fn re owen (L976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77; In re Leqan
(1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; Biondo Advice Letter, No. I-90-543, copies
enc I osed )
In summary, as uayor of Mammoth Lakes, Mayor Alper nay.not
participate in any decision in vhich a source of incorne of S250 or
more in the preceding 12 nonths is directly involved- In
addition, Mayor Alper may not participate in any decision
indi.rect).y aifecting a source of incone of S250 or nore in the
preceding 12 months if the decision wil'I foreseeably and
rnaterial.Iy affect the source of income.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this natter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to
contact ne at (916) 322'5901.
SH : JWW: aa
Enc losure s
sincerely′
scott Hallabrin
Counsel′  Legal DiViSion
